items.title: Importance of Manual Platelet Counting
items.content: Manual examination of platelets in a peripheral blood smear provides critical insights that automated counters may miss, such as the presence of large platelets indicating rapid turnover or marrow production issues.
keyPoints: Use an oil immersion lens to magnify cells 100-fold and estimate platelet count by counting in five to six fields.
Large platelets can indicate rapid turnover or rare inherited syndromes; absence may suggest marrow problems.
Platelet clumping, possibly due to anticoagulants, can lead to falsely low automated counts.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.title: Evaluating Red Cell Morphology
items.content: Assessing red cell size and shape in a blood smear helps diagnose conditions like microcytic or macrocytic anemia, with RDW providing additional diagnostic value.
keyPoints: Compare red cells to the nucleus of small lymphocytes (8-μm wide) for size assessment; smaller indicates microcytosis, larger indicates macrocytosis.
Macrocytic cells are often oval-shaped and called macrovalocytes.
RDW is calculated as (standard deviation of MCV ÷ mean MCV) × 100; normal range is 11–14%, increasing to 15–18% in anisocytosis.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Utility of RDW
items.content: RDW aids in differentiating between types of anemia and has been associated with increased all-cause mortality, making it a valuable diagnostic tool.
keyPoints: In microcytic anemia, iron deficiency shows large size variability and RDW; thalassemia shows uniform small cells with normal RDW.
Elevated RDW can indicate dimorphic anemia from vitamin B12 malabsorption and iron deficiency.
An elevated RDW has been linked to increased all-cause mortality in population studies.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.title: Red Cell Inclusions: Indicators of Underlying Conditions
items.content: The presence of specific inclusions within red blood cells can indicate underlying pathologies or physiological disruptions. For example, basophilic stippling is often associated with lead poisoning due to RNA residue, while Howell-Jolly bodies suggest defective splenic function. Nuclei in red cells imply premature release from the marrow, typically seen in myelophthisic processes or hemolytic anemia.
keyPoints: Basophilic stippling indicates RNA residue and is common in lead poisoning.
Howell-Jolly bodies suggest defective splenic function due to nuclear remnants.
Presence of nuclei implies premature red cell release, often seen in myelophthisis or hemolytic anemia.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.title: Abnormal Red Cell Shapes: Diagnostic Implications
items.content: The shape of red blood cells can provide diagnostic clues. Spherocytes, lacking central pallor, are associated with hereditary spherocytosis and other hemolytic anemias. Dacrocytes, or teardrop-shaped cells, may indicate severe iron deficiency or myelofibrosis. Schistocytes suggest microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, while echinocytes can be artifacts or seen in renal failure.
keyPoints: Spherocytes are linked to hereditary spherocytosis and other hemolytic anemias.
Dacrocytes indicate conditions like severe iron deficiency or myelofibrosis.
Schistocytes reflect microangiopathic hemolytic anemia or mechanical fragmentation.
Echinocytes can be artifacts or seen in renal failure, often reversible.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.question: Which cellular component should be examined first when analyzing a peripheral blood smear?
items.explanation: The author's approach is to look at the smallest cellular elements, the platelets, first.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.question: What can large platelets indicate in a peripheral blood smear?
options: Rapid platelet turnover or certain rare inherited syndromes
items.explanation: Large platelets may be a sign of rapid platelet turnover, as young platelets are often larger than old ones; alternatively, certain rare inherited syndromes can produce large platelets.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.question: What is the significance of an elevated RDW in a patient with microcytic anemia?
options: Shows normal red cell size distribution
items.explanation: In patients with microcytic anemia, a large RDW can suggest iron deficiency as opposed to thalassemia where the small red cells are generally of uniform size.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.question: What is the normal range for RDW?
items.explanation: The normal range for RDW is 11–14%.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following red cell inclusions is characterized by dense blue circular bodies representing nuclear remnants?
items.explanation: Howell-Jolly bodies are dense blue circular inclusions that represent nuclear remnants.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is associated with the presence of spherocytes on a peripheral blood smear?
items.explanation: Spherocytes are small red cells without central pallor and can be seen in hereditary spherocytosis.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.question: What is the significance of Heinz bodies on a peripheral blood smear?
items.explanation: Heinz bodies are precipitated hemoglobin that require vital stains to be visualized.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.statement: Platelet clumping can cause falsely elevated automated platelet counts.
items.explanation: Platelet clumping is associated with falsely low automated platelet counts, not elevated.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.statement: RDW increases in the presence of morphologic anisocytosis.
items.explanation: In the presence of morphologic anisocytosis, RDW increases to 15–18%.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.statement: Red cells become hyperchromic if more than the normal amount of hemoglobin is made.
items.explanation: If more hemoglobin is produced, red cells get larger but do not become darker.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.statement: Schistocytes are associated with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.
items.explanation: Schistocytes reflect microangiopathic hemolytic anemia or fragmentation on artificial heart valves.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 2)

items.assertion: Elevated platelet counts usually signify a myeloproliferative disorder or a reaction to systemic inflammation.
items.reason: Increased platelet production can be due to bone marrow disorders or inflammatory responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)

items.assertion: The absence of platelet granules always indicates marrow disease.
items.reason: Absence of platelet granules may be an artifact or indicate a rare congenital anomaly like gray platelet syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because absence of granules can also be due to handling artifacts.
items.reference: Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears · Interpreting Peripheral Blood Smears (part 1)
